retrieved_docs.title: Genetic and Syndromic Causes of Obesity: Diagnosis and Management -- Introduction -- Obesity Epigenetics
retrieved_docs.content: Epigenetic modifications, eg, deoxyribonucleic acid (DNA) methylation and histone modifications, influence gene expression without altering the underlying DNA sequence. Environmental factors, eg, maternal nutrition during pregnancy, can influence these modifications. For example, maternal undernutrition during pregnancy has been linked to an increased risk of obesity in offspring through epigenetic mechanisms that affect genes involved in metabolism and appetite regulation.

retrieved_docs.content: Based on genome-wide association studies, multiple genes are involved with obesity, and each gene contributes to minor effects promoting obesity. [11] In any given environment, individuals who inherit large gene subsets have a higher tendency to become overweight. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetic and Syndromic Causes of Obesity: Diagnosis and Management -- Introduction -- Red Flags for Genetic Obesity
retrieved_docs.content: Clinicians should request a referral for a genetic evaluation if any of the following red flag indicators are present in patients with obesity: Severe obesity onset before age 5 years Hyperphagia with food-seeking behaviors Obesity accompanied by developmental delays Obesity with distinctive dysmorphic features A family history of severe early-onset obesity Obesity and endocrine abnormalities (eg, hypogonadism and growth hormone deficiency) Obesity and vision problems or polydactyly A failure to respond to standard weight management interventions

retrieved_docs.title: Genetic and Syndromic Causes of Obesity: Diagnosis and Management -- Function -- Prader-Willi syndrome
retrieved_docs.content: Prader-Willi syndrome (PWS) is the most common syndromic cause of obesity, occurring in approximately 1 in 15,000 to 20,000 births. The condition results from the loss of expression of paternally inherited genes on chromosome 15q11-q13. PWS is characterized by neonatal hypotonia followed by hyperphagia, distinctive facial features, and behavioral abnormalities (see Table 3 ). [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Genetic and Syndromic Causes of Obesity: Diagnosis and Management -- Function -- Syndromic Obesity Conditions
retrieved_docs.content: Syndromic forms of obesity, such as Prader-Willi syndrome, Bardet-Biedl syndrome, and Alström syndrome, are characterized by distinctive constellations of clinical features and specific inheritance patterns. Accurate diagnosis requires a high index of suspicion, careful recognition of these phenotypic patterns, and confirmation through targeted genetic testing when indicated.

retrieved_docs.content: The leptin-melanocortin pathway is central to appetite regulation, and its disruption leads to severe obesity accompanied by hyperphagia.

retrieved_docs.title: Genetic and Syndromic Causes of Obesity: Diagnosis and Management -- Other Issues -- Management pearls
retrieved_docs.content: Monogenic and syndromic obesity should be suspected in patients with severe early-onset obesity, especially when accompanied by hyperphagia, developmental delays, or distinctive physical features.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1439
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism represent a broad category of metabolic disturbances that encompass specific conditions such as اضطراب التمثيل الغذائي and فرط البروتينات الشحمية. This category also includes the clinical concept ارتفاع السكر في الدم/داء السكري البادِئُ الجديد (SMQ) as one of its members, indicating a link between lipid abnormalities and early‑onset hyperglycemia. In classification systems, disorders of lipid metabolism are mapped to فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ, a form of familial hyperlipidemia. فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that the trait is transmitted through a single copy of the altered gene from an affected parent. Together, these relationships highlight how lipid metabolism disorders are interconnected with other metabolic disorders, specific disease entities, and genetic inheritance mechanisms, informing both diagnostic categorization and patient‑care strategies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2900
retrieved_docs.content: CONDITIONS OF FETAL GROWTH AND MALNUTRITION represent a collective group of perinatal problems that encompass short gestation, low birth weight, and fetal growth retardation. Short gestation, defined as delivery before full term, often results in low birth weight because the infant has had insufficient time for normal growth. Low birth weight, in turn, frequently reflects fetal growth retardation, a condition in which the fetus does not achieve its genetically expected size. These three entities are closely interrelated, each contributing to the broader spectrum of fetal growth and malnutrition disorders, and together they increase the risk of adverse neonatal outcomes. Effective maternal care that optimizes nutrition and prevents premature delivery is essential for reducing the incidence of these interconnected conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1975
retrieved_docs.content: Disorders of lipid metabolism encompass conditions such as فرط البروتينات الشحمية (hyperlipoproteinemia). Within this broader category, فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ (type IIa hyperlipidemia) is a specific manifestation that is mapped to the class of lipid‑metabolism disorders. Type IIa hyperlipidemia follows an Autosomal dominant inheritance pattern, meaning that Autosomal dominant inheritance is the genetic transmission mechanism for this particular disorder. Recognizing that فرط شحميات الدم من النوع الثاني أ is a form of فرط البروتينات الشحمية and is inherited autosomally dominant helps clinicians diagnose, screen families, and manage patients with lipid metabolism abnormalities.
